musicalLanguage.description: Yiruma's Kiss the Rain runs 96 measures at 130 BPM, and the content strongly contradicts the stated Db major (statedKeyFit -0.594) while the harness's best content-based guess, G major, stays just below the loud-mismatch threshold (margin 0.127) — but the actual chord windows settle the question: C major, E minor, G major, C major seventh, and D major, the most-cited labels among the 28% of windows considered that clear the gate, are exactly the IV, vi, I, IVmaj7, and V of G major, a clean diatonic set. There is no written rest anywhere in these 96 measures. Structurally the piece is built largely from two enormous repeating blocks: a 161-note right-hand passage covers 49.2% of all right-hand events across measures 41-64 and 65-88, while a 178-note left-hand passage covers 41.8% of left-hand events across measures 10-30 and 30-50. On top of those large blocks, a shorter cell recurs 8-9 times in both hands together (for example measures 1-3, 5-7, 9-11, 25-27, and 29-31), each hand's version compressing at an identical 6.545 ratio.
musicalLanguage.structure: A brief 4-measure opening leads into a long developmental span (measures 5-32) built from the 8-9-times-recurring short cell shared by both hands. From measure 41, the piece settles into its two largest structural blocks: the right hand's 161-note passage spans measures 41-64 and repeats measure-for-measure at measures 65-88 (49.2% of all right-hand events), overlapping with the left hand's own 178-note repeating passage across measures 10-30 and 30-50. The section lens's sharpest pivots — measure 81 (novelty 3.268) and measure 57 (2.877) — sit inside this large repeating structure, marking real internal turns even while the pattern pass shows the broader material recurring.
keyMoments: The most-cited chord labels — C major (12 windows), E minor (5), G major (4), C major seventh (3), and D major (3) — are exactly the IV, vi, I, IVmaj7, and V of G major, a clean diatonic set that contradicts the stated Db major even though the whole-song key-detection margin alone (0.127) falls just under the harness's loud-mismatch threshold.

A 161-note right-hand passage covers 49.2% of all right-hand events in the piece, appearing complete at measures 41-64 and again at measures 65-88 — nearly half the right hand's total material lives inside this one repeated block.

A short cell recurs 8 times in the left hand and 9 times in the right hand, largely at the same starting measures in both (for example measures 1-3, 5-7, 9-11, 25-27, and 29-31), each hand's version compressing at an identical 6.545 ratio — this is the piece's true recurring building block, more frequent than either of the two large blocks.
There is no written rest anywhere in the piece's 96 measures — every measure sounds in both hands, so any sense of breathing space has to come from touch and pedaling rather than from silence.
teachingGoals: Learn to hear C major, E minor, G major, and D major as this piece's true diatonic core (the IV, vi, I, and V of G major), rather than trying to relate the harmony back to the stated Db major.

Master the 161-note right-hand passage that covers nearly half of all right-hand material — since it appears complete at both measures 41-64 and 65-88, learning it once handles a huge share of the piece.

Practice the short 8-9-times-recurring cell shared by both hands as the piece's real building block, recognizing it at each of its many appearances rather than relearning it as new material each time.

Build genuine stamina for continuous playing — with no written rest across 96 measures, practice sustaining consistent touch and pedaling from start to finish rather than relying on built-in recovery points.
styleTips: Pedal for resonance in the absence of written rests — since the piece never actually stops, the pedal has to create every sense of phrase-breath the performer wants the listener to feel.
Play the repeated 8-9-times cell with small, deliberate variations in touch across its many appearances — a shape this frequent risks sounding mechanical if played completely identically every time.
Listen for the diatonic C-Em-G-D cycle as the real harmonic home of the piece, and let your ear settle into G major rather than reaching for the stated Db major.

Try building the piece's two large repeating blocks with real endurance in mind — since so much of the piece is these two extended blocks, pace yourself across their full length rather than only the shorter opening material.
